title: Biweekly change in confirmed COVID-19 cases
subtitle: The biweekly change on any given date measures the percentage change in the number of new confirmed cases over the last 14 days, relative to the number in the previous 14 days. Due to limited testing, the number of confirmed cases is lower than the true number of infections.
caption:
Biweekly change in confirmed COVID-19 cases, Feb 22, 2026

The biweekly change on any given date measures the percentage change in the number of new confirmed cases over the last 14 days, relative to the number in the previous 14 days. Due to limited testing, the number of confirmed cases is lower than the true number of infections.

This choropleth map displays the biweekly percentage change in confirmed COVID-19 cases across the globe as of February 22, 2026, using a diverging color scale ranging from deep blue for a 100% decrease to deep red for a 100% increase. The visualization highlights a distinct geographic split in case trends, with the most significant reductions visible in the Americas and parts of Europe. In South America, Peru and Uruguay appear in the darkest blue band, indicating a decline of between 75% and 100% in new cases compared to the prior two weeks. Similarly, in Central America and the Caribbean, Guatemala and Cuba show decreases falling within the -75% to -50% range. Moving to Europe, a cluster of nations including Greece, Italy, and Poland are shaded in medium blue tones, representing drops between 50% and 25%. Conversely, a smaller set of countries shows rising case numbers, depicted in shades of orange and red. Estonia and Lithuania are colored in light orange, corresponding to an increase between 0% and 25%, while Latvia sits in the darker orange band for a 25% to 50% rise. The vast majority of the world’s landmass, including the United States, Canada, Russia, China, Australia, and nearly all of Africa and the Middle East, is covered in diagonal hatching. This pattern signifies that no data is available for these regions on this date. The data shows a concentration of declining infection rates in specific Latin American and Southern European nations, while isolated increases occur in the Baltic region, leaving large portions of the global population without reported figures for this period.
